title: StackScout // Methodology
thesis: StackScout is a public guide, not a benchmark lab. We badge selectively, update from official sources where possible, and avoid pretending every fast-moving tool deserves a fixed numeric score.

principles.title: Curated over exhaustive
principles.body: We start with a smaller cohort of tools that matter, then widen coverage once the update pipeline is trustworthy.

principles.title: Official sources first
principles.body: Release notes, changelogs, product blogs, docs, and official repos outrank secondary coverage.

principles.title: Badges over fake precision
principles.body: Public verdicts use editorial badges and short written theses instead of fragile decimal scoring.

principles.title: Freshness must be visible
principles.body: Every record carries dates. If something has not been updated recently, the site should say that plainly.

badges.label: Recommended
badges.description: Worth active attention now. Strong fit, solid signal, and easy to justify to other builders.

badges.label: Specialist Pick
badges.description: Excellent in a narrower lane, even if it is not the first tool every builder should start with.

badges.label: Worth Watching
badges.description: Not a full recommendation yet, but moving in an interesting enough direction to track.

badges.label: Early but Promising
badges.description: Still forming. Included because the shape is compelling, not because it is already complete.

maturity.label: Stable
maturity.description: Operationally dependable enough to recommend without caveat-heavy framing.

maturity.label: Fast-moving
maturity.description: Shipping quickly, improving visibly, and still changing shape month to month.

maturity.label: Experimental
maturity.description: Interesting, usable in parts, but still clearly in discovery.

maturity.label: Legacy
maturity.description: Still worth knowing about, but no longer where the momentum is.

freshnessPolicy: Tool records carry the date of the latest tracked change that informed the public entry.
Update feed entries carry the source date and source link.
A stale entry is better than a fake live one; honesty beats noise.
reviewLimits: No private operational details in the public layer.
No claims of integration depth we have not verified or sourced.
